elements.sent-2411-001: The Matroid Secretary Problem (MSP) is one of the most prominent settings for online resource allocation and optimal stopping.
elements.sent-2411-002: A decision-maker is presented with a ground set of elements $E$ revealed sequentially and in random order.
elements.sent-2411-003: Upon arrival, an irrevocable decision is made in a take-it-or-leave-it fashion, subject to a feasibility constraint on the set of selected elements captured by a matroid defined over $E$.
elements.sent-2411-004: The decision-maker only has ordinal access to compare the elements, and the goal is to design an algorithm that selects every element of the optimal basis with probability at least $\alpha$ (i.e., $\alpha$-probability-competitive).
elements.sent-2411-005: While the existence of a constant probability-competitive algorithm for MSP remains a major open question, simple greedy policies are at the core of state-of-the-art algorithms for several matroid classes.
elements.sent-2411-006: We introduce a flexible and general algorithmic framework to analyze greedy-like algorithms for MSP based on constructing a language associated with the matroid.
elements.sent-2411-007: Using this language, we establish a lower bound on the probability-competitiveness of the algorithm by studying a corresponding Poisson point process that governs the words' distribution in the language.
elements.sent-2411-008: Using our framework, we break the state-of-the-art guarantee for laminar matroids by settling the probability-competitiveness of the greedy-improving algorithm to be exactly $1-\ln(2)\approx 0.3068$.
elements.sent-2411-009: For graphic matroids, we show a probability-competitiveness of $0.2693$ when the underlying graph has no parallel edges and a guarantee of $0.2504$ for general graphs, also breaking the state-of-the-art factor of $0.25$.
elements.sent-2411-010: One of the most celebrated problems in online decision-making is the secretary problem, in which one aims to hire the best out of $n$ candidates that arrive in a uniformly random order.
elements.sent-2411-011: The decision-maker can only rank the candidates observed up to that point and, upon observing a candidate, must make an immediate and irrevocable decision to accept or reject them.
elements.sent-2411-012: The famous optimal strategy of rejecting the first $1/e$ fraction of candidates and then selecting the first one who is better than all previously seen candidates guarantees at least a $1/e$ probability of hiring the best candidate (see, e.g., Lindley~\cite{lindley , Dynkin~\cite{dynkin ).}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Arguably, one of the most interesting generalizations is the matroid secretary problem (MSP) introduced by Babaioff, Immorlica, Kempe and Kleinberg~\cite{mat-sec .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In the MSP, the decision-maker is given a matroid and can hire any set of candidates forming an independent set in the matroid.
elements.sent-2411-015: An algorithm that, for a fixed optimal basis $B$, selects a set in which every candidate from $B$ appears with probability at least $\alpha$ is called $\alpha$-probability-competitive.
elements.sent-2411-016: For the remainder of this paper, we assume, without loss of generality, that there exists a unique optimal basis.
elements.sent-2411-017: When the decision-maker observes a weight associated with each candidate and the selected set has weight at least an $\alpha$ fraction of the optimal weight basis, the algorithm is $\alpha$-utility-competitive.
elements.sent-2411-018: Over the last two decades, there has been a series of works studying the competitiveness for general matroids, resulting in $\Omega(1/\log{\log{r )$ utility-competitive and $\Omega(1/\log r)$ probability-competitive algorithms~\cite{mat-sec,sqrt-log-r-matroid-sec,loglogrank-matroid-sec1,loglogrank-matroid-sec2,forbidden-paper for rank $r$ matroids.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Babaioff, Immorlica, Kempe and Kleinberg~\cite{mat-sec conjectured the existence of a (utility) constant-competitive algorithm for every matroid.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The matroid secretary conjecture, as it has been known since, has been resolved for several sub-classes of matroids~\cite{forbidden-paper,dinitz-secretary,KesselheimRTV13,korula-pal-graphic,soto-secretary,ImW11 , but it remains open in general and even for fundamental sub-classes such as gammoids or binary matroids.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Equally interesting is the question of the best-possible competitive ratio for matroids, assuming that the matroid secretary conjecture holds.
elements.sent-2411-022: The strong matroid secretary conjecture asserts that there exists a $1/e$-probability-competitive algorithm for all matroids; in other words, that the worst-case for the MSP is the rank-$1$ setting.
elements.sent-2411-023: Surprisingly, the strong matroid secretary conjecture is still wide open for most matroid sub-classes and has been shown to hold only for uniform \cite{klein-secr , partition \cite{mat-sec and transversal matroids \cite{kesselheim-transversal-mat-sec .}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Perhaps the simplest matroid class for which the strong matroid secretary conjecture is still open is the class of rank-$2$ matroids, which fall under the class of laminar matroids and for which the best-known algorithm prior to our work is a $0.2587$-utility-competitive algorithm \cite{zahra-laminar-secretary .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Motivated by the strong matroid secretary conjecture, our work aims to shorten the gap between the known competitiveness guarantees and $1/e$ for several matroid sub-classes and add to the toolbox of techniques that can be used to yield improved guarantees in the future.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this work, we develop new tools for analyzing algorithms for the MSP that are more effective at managing the complex correlations involved in determining whether an element arriving at a specific time can be selected.
elements.sent-2411-027: Conceptually, our main contribution is introducing a new framework to analyze greedy-improving algorithms for the MSP using labeling schemes.
elements.sent-2411-028: Using this, we improve the state-of-the-art probability-competitiveness for the fundamental laminar and graphic matroid classes and match the best-known probability-competitiveness guarantees for several other matroids as we extend the capabilities of the forbidden sets framework by Soto, Turkieltaub, and Verdugo~\cite{forbidden-paper (see Appendix~\ref{app:qforbidden ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The algorithms we analyze initially skip the first $p$ fraction of elements.
elements.sent-2411-030: Afterwards, they mark every element that improves upon the current best set, i.e., an improving element.
elements.sent-2411-031: Some marked elements will be added to the output $\ALG$, when they arrive, according to an internal property $P$ specific to the matroid.
elements.sent-2411-032: Property $P$ filters the improving elements and guarantees the feasibility of the resulting selection, i.e., if $P$ holds when an element $e$ arrives, then it must be that $\ALG \cup \{e\}$ is independent.
elements.sent-2411-033: To analyze the algorithm, we consider a separate procedure, called a labeling scheme that visits the marked elements in reverse order and assigns a label to each.
elements.sent-2411-034: At the end of the procedure, we obtain a word $z$ by concatenating all labels assigned by our scheme.
elements.sent-2411-035: The core idea is that, for every matroid $\cM$, we can assign a language $\cL(\cM)$ such that, for any fixed element $e^*$ in the optimal set, $\Pr[e^* \in \ALG] \geq \Pr[z \in \cL(\cM)]$, where $\ALG$ is the final set of our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-036: Then, our competitiveness guarantees follow by computing $\Pr[z \in \cL(\cM)]$, and we show that the uniformly random arrival order of the elements implies that the characters (i.e., the labels) of $z$ are chosen uniformly at random from the label set and the length of $z$ follows a Poisson distribution.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our new algorithmic framework using labeling schemes is described in detail in Section~\ref{sec:label .}
elements.sent-2411-038: One of the first algorithms proposed for the MSP was the greedy-improving algorithm, which greedily selects every improving element, subject to feasibility.
elements.sent-2411-039: Despite its simplicity, it has been remarkably successful for many matroid classes.
elements.sent-2411-040: For laminar matroids, the best-known guarantee before our work was the very recent $\approx 0.2105$-probability-competitiveness, achieved by Huang, Parsaeian, and Zhu~\cite{zahra-laminar-secretary using the greedy-improving algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Using our labeling scheme framework, we settle the probability-competitiveness of the greedy-improving algorithm for laminar matroids and show a tight guarantee of $1-\ln(2)\approx 0.3068$.
elements.sent-2411-042: In addition, for every $\eps > 0$, there exists a laminar matroid $\cM(\eps)$ such that the probability-competitive ratio of the greedy-improving algorithm on $\cM(\eps)$ is at most $1 - \ln(2) + \eps$.
elements.sent-2411-043: For every natural number $r$, we characterize the optimal competitiveness attained by the greedy-improving algorithm on laminar matroids of rank $r$.
elements.sent-2411-044: The competitiveness decreases as $r \to \infty$, with a limit of $1 - \ln(2)$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We also obtain a full characterization of the greedy-improving algorithm on uniform matroids for any fixed rank $r$ and show that it grows from $1/e$ when $r=1$ to $1-1/e$ as $r\to \infty$ (Proposition \ref{lem:gi ).}
elements.sent-2411-046: Working towards an answer for the strong matroid secretary conjecture, we study the simplest matroids for which the existence of a $1/e$-probability-competitive algorithm is still open -- rank-$2$ matroids.

elements.sent-2411-107: We provide a few basic definitions for matroids and refer the reader to~\cite{oxley2011matroid for an extensive treatment.}
elements.sent-2411-108: A {\it matroid $\calM = (E, \calI)$ is defined by its {\it ground set $E$ and its {\it family of independent sets $\calI\subseteq 2^E$ that satisfies the so-called {\it independence axioms : (I1) $\emptyset\in\calI$, (I2) $X\subseteq Y,\ Y\in\calI\Rightarrow X\in\calI$, and (I3) $X,Y\in\calI,\ |X|<|Y|\Rightarrow\exists e\in Y-X\ \text{such that X+e\in\calI$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-113: The {\it rank function of the matroid is a function $r_\cM: 2^E \to \NN$, where $r_\cM(S)$ denotes the size of a maximal independent set contained in $S$.}

elements.section-2411-008: Forbidden Sets Technique through Labeling Schemes
elements.sent-2411-878: We briefly describe the $q$-forbidden technique of Soto, Turkieltaub, and Verdugo~\cite{forbidden-paper and show how to implement every $q$-forbidden algorithm via a labeling scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-879: For every matroid class $\cC$ that admits a $q$-forbidden algorithm $\ALG$, there exists a labeling scheme $\Lambda$ such that, for every $p \in (0,1)$ and $e^*\in \OPT$, \begin{equation}\Pr[e^* \in \ALG] =  -p \ln(p) for $q = 1$, {(p-p^q)}/{(q-1)} for $q \geq 2$. .\end{equation}.
